name: ध्यानयोग
teaching: The mind must be befriended before it can be stilled: for the one who has conquered it, the mind is the closest ally; for the one who hasn't, it behaves like an enemy. Krishna gives the practice — seat, posture, moderation, patient return — and the image: a lamp in a windless place, unflickering. Even the practitioner who falls short loses nothing; no sincere effort on this path is ever wasted. The seat, the breath, the moderated life, the lamp in the windless place — meditation is given its complete manual, and then its safety net: no effort on this path is ever lost, across lifetimes. Its summit is the yogi who loves.
questions: Right now, is your mind your friend or your opponent?
Where is your life immoderate — in eating, sleeping, effort, rest — in ways that make stillness impossible?
Arjuna admits the mind is as hard to hold as the wind. What is your actual, daily relationship with practice and patience?

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
अनाश्रितः कर्मफलं कार्यं कर्म करोति यः ।
स संन्यासी च योगी च न निरग्निर्न चाक्रियः॥
verses.transliteration:
anāśritaḥ karmaphalaṃ kāryaṃ karma karoti yaḥ
sa saṃnyāsī ca yogī ca na niragnirna cākriyaḥ
verses.essence: One who does the work that must be done without leaning on its fruit is the true sannyasi and yogi — not the one without fire or rites.
verses.translation: He who performs his bounden duty without depending on the fruits of his actions he is a Sannyasi and a Yogi; not he who is without fire and without action.

anvaya.meaning: not depending on action's fruit
who does the work that ought to be done
that one is sannyasi and yogi
not the fireless one, nor the actionless

verses.explanation: The chapter opens by revoking renunciation's badges: no sacred fire, no rituals — these externals prove nothing. The double title goes instead to whoever still does the needful while no longer leaning on what it yields. Renunciation is measured at the point of motivation, not at the doorstep of the monastery.
verses.connection: Carries chapter 5's verdict forward: the working detached one holds both titles at once.
verses.reflection: Do one duty today expecting nothing back — and notice you have already begun renunciation.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
यं संन्यासमिति प्राहुर्योगं तं विद्धि पाण्डव ।
न ह्यसंन्यस्तसङ्कल्पो योगी भवति कश्चन॥
verses.transliteration:
yaṃ saṃnyāsamiti prāhuryogaṃ taṃ viddhi pāṇḍava
na hyasaṃnyastasaṅkalpo yogī bhavati kaścana
verses.essence: What they call renunciation, know that as yoga, Pandava — for no one becomes a yogi without renouncing self-will.
verses.translation: Do thou, O Arjuna, know Yoga to be that which they call renunciation; no one verily becomes a Yogi who has not renounced thoughts.

anvaya.meaning: what they call sannyasa
know that as yoga, O Pandava
for without renouncing sankalpa
no one becomes a yogi

verses.explanation: The two words are formally merged, and the common root is named: saṅkalpa — the mind's self-willed projecting of plans and cravings. Drop that, and renunciation and yoga turn out to be one act seen from two sides. What must be given up was never the work; it was the scheming around it.
verses.connection: Seals 6.1's equation with its mechanism: the shared price of both paths is sankalpa.
verses.reflection: Find the resolve hiding inside your calmest practice; sannyasa and yoga share one engine.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
आरुरुक्षोर्मुनेर्योगं कर्म कारणमुच्यते ।
योगारूढस्य तस्यैव शमः कारणमुच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
ārurukṣormuneryogaṃ karma kāraṇamucyate
yogārūḍhasya tasyaiva śamaḥ kāraṇamucyate
verses.essence: For the sage climbing toward yoga, action is the means; for the one who has reached yoga, serenity is the means.
verses.translation: For a sage who wishes to attain to Yoga, action is said to be the means; for the same sage who has attained to Yoga, inaction (iescence) is said to be the means.

anvaya.meaning: for the sage desiring to ascend to yoga
action is said to be the means
for that same one, mounted on yoga
serenity is said to be the means

verses.explanation: One path, two gears. The climber needs karma — engagement that scrubs and steadies; the arrived needs śama — quiet that deepens what engagement built. The error is using the wrong gear for one's actual altitude: premature stillness stalls, perpetual busyness never summits.
verses.connection: Resolves the act-versus-sit tension as a question of stage, not of doctrine.
verses.reflection: Know your stage honestly today: still climbing needs action; arrived needs quiet. Prescribe accordingly.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
यदा हि नेन्द्रियार्थेषु न कर्मस्वनुषज्जते ।
सर्वसङ्कल्पसंन्यासी योगारूढस्तदोच्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā hi nendriyārtheṣu na karmasvanuṣajjate
sarvasaṅkalpasaṃnyāsī yogārūḍhastadocyate
verses.essence: When one clings neither to sense-objects nor to actions, having renounced all self-will, then one is called mounted on yoga.
verses.translation: When a man is not attached to the sense-objects or to actions, having renounced all thoughts, then he is said to have attained to Yoga.

anvaya.meaning: when indeed neither to sense-objects
nor to actions one clings
renouncer of all sankalpa
then one is called mounted on yoga

verses.explanation: The summit-test for 6.3's gear-change: not just sense-objects released, but the subtler clinging to activity itself — the doer's addiction to doing. When both hooks are out and self-will has gone quiet, the climb is over and the sitting may rightly begin.
verses.connection: Defines yogārūḍha precisely, so no one shifts to stillness on self-flattery.
verses.reflection: Mark a small milestone: one old craving that no longer argues when you pass it.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. verses.devanagari:
उद्धरेदात्मनात्मानं नात्मानमवसादयेत् ।
आत्मैव ह्यात्मनो बन्धुरात्मैव रिपुरात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
uddharedātmanātmānaṃ nātmānamavasādayet
ātmaiva hyātmano bandhurātmaiva ripurātmanaḥ
verses.essence: Let one lift the self by the self; let one not degrade the self. For the self alone is the self's friend, and the self alone its enemy.
verses.translation: One should raise oneself by one's Self alone; let not one lower oneself; for the Self alone is the friend of oneself, and the Self alone is the enemy of oneself.

anvaya.meaning: let one raise the self by the self
let one not sink the self
for the self alone is the self's friend
the self alone the self's enemy

verses.explanation: The Gita's psychology in one couplet: no rescuer and no saboteur ultimately stands outside you. The same instrument — mind, will, habit — is rope or weight depending on how it is held. Responsibility this total is alarming for a moment, then liberating forever: the decisive relationship of your life is already in the room. Uddhared atmanatmanam — let one lift oneself by oneself: the Gita's charter of self-responsibility, and the commentaries immediately ask which 'self' lifts which. The settled reading: the higher mind rescues the lower, the befriended will lifts the entangled one. Atmaiva hy atmano bandhuh — no rescue arrives that the inner ally has not first agreed to.
verses.connection: Grounds the whole meditation chapter: before posture and breath, the self must take its own side.
verses.schools_note: 'Atmaiva hy atmano bandhuh' — the self as its own friend or enemy: commentators parse the two 'selves' as higher mind and lower (Shankara), or the soul and its embodied instrument (Ramanuja). The theistic schools add the balancing doctrine: self-effort opens the door grace walks through — 6.47 and 18.62 complete what this verse begins. None read it as excluding help; all read it as excluding excuse.
verses.reflection: Lift yourself by yourself once today — one act your future self would thank you for, unprompted.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
बन्धुरात्मात्मनस्तस्य येनात्मैवात्मना जितः ।
अनात्मनस्तु शत्रुत्वे वर्तेतात्मैव शत्रुवत्॥
verses.transliteration:
bandhurātmātmanastasya yenātmaivātmanā jitaḥ
anātmanastu śatrutve vartetātmaiva śatruvat
verses.essence: For one who has conquered the self, the self is a friend; for the unconquered, the self behaves like an enemy.
verses.translation: The Self is the friend of the self of him by whom the self has been conered by the Self, but to the unconered self, this Self stands in the position of an enemy, like an (external) foe.

anvaya.meaning: the self is the friend of that self
by whom the self has been conquered by the self
but for the un-self-mastered, in enmity
the self itself would act like an enemy

verses.explanation: The condition on 6.5's friendship: it must be won. An untrained mind is not neutral — it actively works against its owner, śatruvat, *like* an enemy, sabotaging resolutions and amplifying fears. The first conquest of any contemplative life is this internal alliance. Bandhur atmatmanas tasya — the same mind, friend or foe, by one variable: whether it has been conquered into alliance. The tradition reads the verse as ending all blame: no demon required, no fate — the entire case turns on a domestic negotiation. Shatruvat, 'like an enemy': the unconquered mind does not merely fail you; it campaigns.
verses.connection: Sharpens 6.5: friend and enemy are not two selves but one self, before and after mastery.
verses.reflection: Check the alliance: is your mind working for you today, or running its own administration?
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
जितात्मनः प्रशान्तस्य परमात्मा समाहितः ।
शीतोष्णसुखदुःखेषु तथा मानापमानयोः॥
verses.transliteration:
jitātmanaḥ praśāntasya paramātmā samāhitaḥ
śītoṣṇasukhaduḥkheṣu tathā mānāpamānayoḥ
verses.essence: For the self-conquered and serene, the supreme Self stands steady — in cold and heat, joy and sorrow, honor and disgrace.
verses.translation: The Supreme Self of him who is self-controlled and peaceful is balanced in cold and heat, pleasure and pain, as also in honour and dishonour.

anvaya.meaning: of the self-conquered, the serene
the supreme Self is steadfast, composed
in cold-heat, pleasure-pain
likewise in honor and dishonor

verses.explanation: What the won alliance feels like: an inner presence that no longer wobbles with the weather. The three test-pairs ascend in difficulty — body (cold-heat), feeling (joy-sorrow), and the hardest, social standing (honor-disgrace). Steadiness through the last is the rarest coin.
verses.connection: Shows 6.6's victory cashed out: the conquered self becomes a still platform for the Supreme.
verses.reflection: In heat or cold, praise or slight today, try staying the same temperature inside.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
ज्ञानविज्ञानतृप्तात्मा कूटस्थो विजितेन्द्रियः ।
युक्त इत्युच्यते योगी समलोष्टाश्मकाञ्चनः॥
verses.transliteration:
jñānavijñānatṛptātmā kūṭastho vijitendriyaḥ
yukta ityucyate yogī samaloṣṭāśmakāñcanaḥ
verses.essence: Content in knowledge and realization, unshakable, with senses subdued — such a yogi is called harnessed, to whom clod, stone and gold are alike.
verses.translation: The Yogi who is satisfied with the knowledge and the wisdom (of the Self), who has conered the senses, and to whom a clod of earth, a piece of stone and gold are the same, is said to be harmonied (i.e., is said to have attained Nirvikalpa Samadhi).

anvaya.meaning: whose self is content with knowledge and realization
unmoving as an anvil, senses fully conquered
is called yoked, a yogi
equal toward clod, stone and gold

verses.explanation: Two kinds of knowing must both be present: jñāna, the learned map, and vijñāna, the terrain seen firsthand — contentment requires the second. Kūṭastha, anvil-still: the world hammers, the form holds. And the famous triad — dirt-clod, stone, gold — measures freedom by the wallet's loosened grip.
verses.connection: Continues the portrait of 6.7: inner steadiness shown against value itself.
verses.reflection: Look once at gold and once at gravel with the same eyes — just to feel what the sages feel.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
सुहृन्मित्रार्युदासीनमध्यस्थद्वेष्यबन्धुषु ।
साधुष्वपि च पापेषु समबुद्धिर्विशिष्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
suhṛnmitrāryudāsīnamadhyasthadveṣyabandhuṣu
sādhuṣvapi ca pāpeṣu samabuddhirviśiṣyate
verses.essence: One who is equal-minded toward well-wishers, friends, foes, neutrals, mediators, the hateful, kin, and toward saints and sinners alike — excels.
verses.translation: He who is of the same mind to the good-hearted, friends, enemies, the indifferent, the neutral, the hateful, the relatives, the righteous and the unrighteous, excels.

anvaya.meaning: toward well-wishers, friends, enemies, neutrals
mediators, the hateful, and relatives
also toward the virtuous and the sinful
one of equal mind excels

verses.explanation: The hardest equality: people. Nine categories — the whole social spectrum from the one who loves you to the one who hates you, from saint to sinner — met with sama-buddhi, an evenness of regard. Not blindness to difference, but a refusal to let difference dictate the heart's temperature.
verses.connection: Completes 6.7–8: equanimity proven against things, then against persons — the steeper slope.
verses.reflection: Apply one even-mindedness today across the hardest span: well-wisher, stranger, and someone who hurt you.
verses.background: Krishna continues unbroken into the discipline of meditation, first redefining its candidate: the true sannyasi-yogi is the one who does his duty without leaning on results. He gives the ladder — action the means for the climber, serenity for the arrived — and the famous charter of self-rescue: lift yourself by yourself; the mind is friend or foe.

verses.devanagari:
योगी युञ्जीत सततमात्मानं रहसि स्थितः ।
एकाकी यतचित्तात्मा निराशीरपरिग्रहः॥
verses.transliteration:
yogī yuñjīta satatamātmānaṃ rahasi sthitaḥ
ekākī yatacittātmā nirāśīraparigrahaḥ
verses.essence: Let the yogi constantly discipline the self, remaining in solitude, alone, with mind and body controlled, free of longing and possession.
verses.translation: Let the Yogi try constantly to keep the mind steady, remaining in solitude, alone, with the mind and the body controlled, and free from hope and covetousness.

anvaya.meaning: let the yogi constantly yoke
the self, remaining in solitude
alone, with mind and self restrained
without longing, without possessions

verses.explanation: The manual begins. Four conditions before a single breath of technique: solitude, aloneness (related but distinct — one can be solitary yet mentally crowded), restraint, and the two great unburdenings — no expectations, no holdings. Meditation starts by emptying the hands.
verses.connection: Opens the practical core (6.10–15): the portrait of 6.7–9 now taught as a discipline.
verses.reflection: Take ten minutes alone today — no phone, no audience, no inventory — and simply sit with yourself.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
शुचौ देशे प्रतिष्ठाप्य स्थिरमासनमात्मनः ।
नात्युच्छ्रितं नातिनीचं चैलाजिनकुशोत्तरम्॥
verses.transliteration:
śucau deśe pratiṣṭhāpya sthiramāsanamātmanaḥ
nātyucchritaṃ nātinīcaṃ cailājinakuśottaram
verses.essence: In a clean place, having set a firm seat for oneself — neither too high nor too low — of cloth, deerskin and kusha grass.
verses.translation: In a clean spot, having established a firm seat of his own, neither too high nor too low, made of a cloth, a skin and Kusa-grass, one over the other.

anvaya.meaning: in a clean spot, having established
a firm seat of one's own
not too high, not too low
with cloth, skin, and kusha grass layered

verses.explanation: The teaching descends, charmingly, to furniture — because the body's circumstances are the mind's first weather. Clean, stable, neither elevated (pride, precariousness) nor sunken (lethargy, damp): the middle height is already a metaphor the posture will repeat.
verses.connection: First of the concrete instructions: the outer seat prepared before the inner one.
verses.reflection: Prepare your seat with care tonight; the body honors what the hands arrange deliberately.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
तत्रैकाग्रं मनः कृत्वा यतचित्तेन्द्रियक्रियः ।
उपविश्यासने युञ्ज्याद्योगमात्मविशुद्धये॥
verses.transliteration:
tatraikāgraṃ manaḥ kṛtvā yatacittendriyakriyaḥ
upaviśyāsane yuñjyādyogamātmaviśuddhaye
verses.essence: There, making the mind one-pointed, with thought and senses controlled, seated on the seat, let one practice yoga for self-purification.
verses.translation: There, having made the mind one-pointed, with the actions of the mind and the senses controlled, let him, seated on the seat, practise Yoga for the purification of the self.

anvaya.meaning: there, making the mind one-pointed
with the activities of mind and senses restrained
sitting on the seat, let one practice
yoga, for the purification of the self

verses.explanation: The aim is stamped on the practice before it starts: ātma-viśuddhaye, for purification — not for powers, visions, or even peace as a possession. Ekāgra, one-pointed: the mind's usual spray of attention gathered to a single beam. Everything that follows serves that gathering.
verses.connection: Names the motive that keeps 6.10–14's technique honest: cleansing, not acquiring.
verses.reflection: Sit once today with mind gathered to a single point, even briefly — purification needs only minutes.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
समं कायशिरोग्रीवं धारयन्नचलं स्थिरः ।
सम्प्रेक्ष्य नासिकाग्रं स्वं दिशश्चानवलोकयन्॥
verses.transliteration:
samaṃ kāyaśirogrīvaṃ dhārayannacalaṃ sthiraḥ
samprekṣya nāsikāgraṃ svaṃ diśaścānavalokayan
verses.essence: Holding body, head and neck erect and still, gazing at the tip of the nose, not looking around —
verses.translation: Let him firmly hold his body, head and neck erect and still, gazing at the tip of his nose, without looking around.

anvaya.meaning: even the body, head and neck
holding motionless, steady
gazing at the tip of one's own nose
and not looking around at the directions

verses.explanation: The spine as plumb-line: body, head, neck stacked sama, even — alertness built into architecture. The nose-tip gaze is a leash for the eyes, the most restless of the senses; diśaś cānavalokayan, no glancing at the directions, closes the world's ten doors of distraction.
verses.connection: The posture of 6.11–12 completed upward: stillness made structural.
verses.reflection: Hold the spine quietly tall for two minutes; let posture teach steadiness to the mind.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
प्रशान्तात्मा विगतभीर्ब्रह्मचारिव्रते स्थितः ।
मनः संयम्य मच्चित्तो युक्त आसीत मत्परः॥
verses.transliteration:
praśāntātmā vigatabhīrbrahmacārivrate sthitaḥ
manaḥ saṃyamya maccitto yukta āsīta matparaḥ
verses.essence: Serene, fearless, firm in the vow of brahmacharya, with mind restrained and thinking of me, let one sit harnessed, holding me supreme.
verses.translation: Serene-minded, fearless, firm in the vow of a Brahmachari, having controlled the mind, thinking of Me and balanced in mind, let him sit, having Me as his supreme goal.

anvaya.meaning: of serene self, with fear departed
established in the vow of brahmacharya
restraining the mind, with thought on me
let one sit yoked, holding me supreme

verses.explanation: The inner posture to match the outer: serenity, and — easy to miss — vigata-bhīḥ, fearlessness, for the silence of meditation surfaces what noise kept down. Then the object: mat-paraḥ, me as the highest. The Gita's meditation is not on emptiness but on a Person; attention needs a home, and the home offered is Krishna.
verses.connection: Crowns the technique: posture and breath were scaffolding; the dwelling-place is Him.
verses.reflection: Sit with fear set down and vows picked up — serene, brahmachari in attention, aimed at the highest.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी नियतमानसः ।
शान्तिं निर्वाणपरमां मत्संस्थामधिगच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
yuñjannevaṃ sadātmānaṃ yogī niyatamānasaḥ
śāntiṃ nirvāṇaparamāṃ matsaṃsthāmadhigacchati
verses.essence: Thus ever harnessing the self, the yogi of disciplined mind attains the peace that culminates in nirvana and abides in me.
verses.translation: Thus always keeping the mind balanced, the Yogi, with the mind controlled, attains to the peace abiding in Me, which culminates in liberation.

anvaya.meaning: thus always yoking the self
the yogi of regulated mind
the peace whose culmination is nirvana
abiding in me, attains

verses.explanation: The practice section closes with its destination, and the destination has an address: śāntiṁ... mat-saṁsthām — a peace that doesn't trail off into the abstract but settles *in me*. Nirvana, the great extinguishing, turns out to be an arrival as much as a cessation. Note sadā: not a retreat's worth of yoga but a life's.
verses.connection: Seals 6.10–14 with the promise — peace whose far edge is union.
verses.reflection: Practice small but practice always; nirvana is built of ordinary, repeated sittings.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
नात्यश्नतस्तु योगोऽस्ति न चैकान्तमनश्नतः ।
न चातिस्वप्नशीलस्य जाग्रतो नैव चार्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
nātyaśnatastu yogo'sti na caikāntamanaśnataḥ
na cātisvapnaśīlasya jāgrato naiva cārjuna
verses.essence: Yoga is not for the one who eats too much, nor for the one who fasts to extremes; not for the over-sleeper, Arjuna, nor for the ever-wakeful.
verses.translation: Verily Yoga is not possible for him who eats too much, nor for him who does not eat at all, nor for him who sleeps too much, nor for him who is (always) awake, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: yoga is not for the over-eater
nor for one wholly abstaining from food
nor for one given to too much sleep
nor indeed for the (ever) wakeful, Arjuna

verses.explanation: A verse of immense kindness to the body: four extremes disqualified, two of them ascetic. Heroic fasting and vigil are rejected as firmly as indulgence — both are violence to the instrument, and a damaged instrument plays no music. The Gita's yoga refuses to be impressed by self-harm.
verses.connection: Pre-empts the zealot's misreading of 6.10–15: discipline, yes; mortification, no.
verses.reflection: Check your extremes today: too much food or none, too much sleep or none — yoga lives between.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
युक्ताहारविहारस्य युक्तचेष्टस्य कर्मसु ।
युक्तस्वप्नावबोधस्य योगो भवति दुःखहा॥
verses.transliteration:
yuktāhāravihārasya yuktaceṣṭasya karmasu
yuktasvapnāvabodhasya yogo bhavati duḥkhahā
verses.essence: For one moderate in food and recreation, moderate in effort in actions, moderate in sleep and waking — yoga becomes the destroyer of sorrow.
verses.translation: Yoga becomes the destroyer of pain for him who is moderate in eating and recreation (such as walking, etc.), who is moderate in exertion in actions, who is moderate in sleep and wakefulness.

anvaya.meaning: of one of moderate food and recreation
of moderate exertion in actions
of moderate sleep and waking
yoga becomes the slayer of sorrow

verses.explanation: The positive law after 6.16's prohibitions: yukta four times — measured, fitted, tuned. Eating, playing, working, sleeping: the whole round of an ordinary day calibrated to the middle. And only on that humble foundation does the grand promise stand: duḥkha-hā, sorrow-slayer. Transcendence rests on schedule.
verses.connection: The middle way stated in the Gita's own words — moderation as yoga's load-bearing wall.
verses.reflection: Tune one habit toward moderation today and watch how much pain was self-scheduled.
verses.background: The meditation manual proper begins, spoken on a battlefield about a solitude far from it: the secluded place, the firm seat neither too high nor too low, the upright body, the gaze at the nose-tip, the vowed and fearless mind — and the rule of moderation in food, sleep, and effort.

verses.devanagari:
यदा विनियतं चित्तमात्मन्येवावतिष्ठते ।
निःस्पृहः सर्वकामेभ्यो युक्त इत्युच्यते तदा॥
verses.transliteration:
yadā viniyataṃ cittamātmanyevāvatiṣṭhate
niḥspṛhaḥ sarvakāmebhyo yukta ityucyate tadā
verses.essence: When the disciplined mind rests in the Self alone, free of longing for any desire, then one is said to be harnessed.
verses.translation: When the perfectly controlled mind rests in the Self only, free from longing for all the objects of desires, then it is said, 'He is united'.

anvaya.meaning: when the well-regulated mind
rests in the Self alone
without longing for all desires
then one is said to be yoked

verses.explanation: The checkpoint: the mind no longer pacing its cage but settled — avatiṣṭhate, taking up residence — in the Self. The test is niḥspṛha, the absence of that low hum of wanting that usually runs beneath all thought. When the hum stops, the word yukta finally applies.
verses.connection: Defines arrival for the practice of 6.10–17, before the famous image that pictures it.
verses.reflection: Notice an hour when the mind rests in the Self and cravings simply do not report for duty.
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
यथा दीपो निवातस्थो नेङ्गते सोपमा स्मृता ।
योगिनो यतचित्तस्य युञ्जतो योगमात्मनः॥
verses.transliteration:
yathā dīpo nivātastho neṅgate sopamā smṛtā
yogino yatacittasya yuñjato yogamātmanaḥ
verses.essence: As a lamp in a windless place does not flicker — that is the likeness recalled of the yogi of disciplined mind, absorbed in the yoga of the Self.
verses.translation: As a lamp placed in a windless spot does not flicker to such is compared the Yogi of controlled mind, practising Yoga in the Self (or absorbed in the Yoga of the Self).

anvaya.meaning: as a lamp standing in a windless place
does not flicker — that simile is recalled
of the yogi of restrained mind
practicing the yoga of the Self

verses.explanation: Perhaps the most beloved image in all meditation literature. The flame is not extinguished — consciousness burns at full brightness — only the flickering stops, because the wind of desire and distraction has been stilled. Steadiness without dimming: alertness and peace, finally in the same body. Yatha dipo nivata-sthah — as a lamp in a windless place does not flicker: the simile the whole meditation tradition adopted as its flag. Commentators note its double teaching: the flame is attention, the wind is desire's draft — and the practice is less about strengthening the flame than about finding, or building, the windless room.
verses.connection: The emblem of 6.18's settled mind — the chapter's lamp held up for all later tradition.
verses.reflection: Find your windless place today — the corner of the day where the flame of attention stops flickering.
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
यत्रोपरमते चित्तं निरुद्धं योगसेवया ।
यत्र चैवात्मनात्मानं पश्यन्नात्मनि तुष्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
yatroparamate cittaṃ niruddhaṃ yogasevayā
yatra caivātmanātmānaṃ paśyannātmani tuṣyati
verses.essence: Where the mind comes to rest, restrained by the practice of yoga, and where, beholding the Self by the self, one is content in the Self —
verses.translation: When the mind, restrained by the practice of Yoga attains to quietude and when seeing the Self by the self, he is satisfied in his own Self.

anvaya.meaning: where the mind comes to rest
restrained by the service of yoga
and where, by the self, the Self
beholding, one is content in the Self

verses.explanation: A four-verse definition of the goal begins. First mark: uparamate, the mind doesn't just slow — it comes to rest, the way water stills when the stirring stops. In that stillness the impossible-sounding event occurs: the self sees the Self, awareness catching its own light, and contentment needs no further object.
verses.connection: Begins the great description (6.20–23) of what the windless lamp actually illuminates.
verses.reflection: Touch the moment in stillness where the self, seeing itself, is quietly satisfied.
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
सुखमात्यन्तिकं यत्तद् बुद्धिग्राह्यमतीन्द्रियम् ।
वेत्ति यत्र न चैवायं स्थितश्चलति तत्त्वतः॥
verses.transliteration:
sukhamātyantikaṃ yattad buddhigrāhyamatīndriyam
vetti yatra na caivāyaṃ sthitaścalati tattvataḥ
verses.essence: Where one knows that boundless happiness which is grasped by the understanding, beyond the senses — and established there, never strays from the truth —
verses.translation: When he (the Yogi) feels that Infinite Bliss which can be grasped by the (pure) intellect and which transcends the senses, and established wherein he never moves from the Reality.

anvaya.meaning: that happiness which is ultimate, boundless
graspable by the buddhi, beyond the senses

anvaya.word: वेत्ति यत्र न च एव अयं
anvaya.meaning: where one knows (it), and never this one
established, moves from the truth

verses.explanation: Second mark: a happiness with strange specifications — atīndriya, off the sensory grid entirely, yet buddhi-grāhya, apprehensible by the refined understanding. Most joy is delivered through the senses and leaves with them; this one is met by a deeper faculty and therefore cannot be confiscated.
verses.connection: Names the positive content of 6.20's rest: not blankness but trans-sensory bliss.
verses.reflection: Trust the joy beyond the senses once felt — let one taste of depth recalibrate all your sweets.
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
यं लब्ध्वा चापरं लाभं मन्यते नाधिकं ततः ।
यस्मिन्स्थितो न दुःखेन गुरुणापि विचाल्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
yaṃ labdhvā cāparaṃ lābhaṃ manyate nādhikaṃ tataḥ
yasminsthito na duḥkhena guruṇāpi vicālyate
verses.essence: Gaining which, one reckons no other gain greater; established in which, one is not shaken even by the heaviest sorrow —
verses.translation: Which, having obtained, he thinks there is no other gain superior to it; wherein estabished, he is not moved even by heavy sorrow.

anvaya.meaning: gaining which, another gain
one deems not greater than it
established in which, not by sorrow
even heavy, is one shaken

verses.explanation: Third mark, the double test: against all possible gains, this one ends the search — the comparing mind retires; and against guru duḥkha, grief of the heaviest grade, it holds. Not painlessness — the Gita never promises that — but unshakenness: sorrow arrives and finds no purchase on the foundation.
verses.connection: The goal of 6.20–21 stress-tested at both extremes — supreme gain, supreme loss.
verses.reflection: Recall your steadiest moment and ask: established there, what calamity could really move me?
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
तं विद्याद् दुःखसंयोगवियोगं योगसंज्ञितम् ।
स निश्चयेन योक्तव्यो योगोऽनिर्विण्णचेतसा॥
verses.transliteration:
taṃ vidyād duḥkhasaṃyogaviyogaṃ yogasaṃjñitam
sa niścayena yoktavyo yogo'nirviṇṇacetasā
verses.essence: Know that as yoga: the unyoking from union with sorrow. It must be practiced with resolve, with a mind undismayed.
verses.translation: Let that be known by the name of Yoga, the severance from union with pain. This Yoga should be practised with determination and with an undesponding mind.

anvaya.meaning: know that — from the union with sorrow
the disjunction — as named yoga
that (yoga) must be practiced with determination
with a mind free of despondency

verses.explanation: The chapter's definitional jewel, built on a pun: yoga, 'union', defined as viyoga, 'dis-union' — the surgical separation from our oldest attachment, the one to suffering itself. Then two clinical instructions for the long operation: niścaya, settled resolve, and anirviṇṇa-cetas — a heart that has renounced discouragement.
verses.connection: Caps 6.20–22 by naming the whole achievement, and pivots to the labor it costs.
verses.reflection: Redefine yoga today as Krishna does: the unlinking from your link with sorrow. Practice with resolve.
verses.background: Krishna describes the state the practice ripens into: the disciplined mind like a lamp in a windless place; the self beholding the self, satisfied; the joy beyond the senses from which one does not waver — and gives his deepest definition: yoga is the unyoking from union with pain.

verses.devanagari:
सङ्कल्पप्रभवान्कामांस्त्यक्त्वा सर्वानशेषतः ।
मनसैवेन्द्रियग्रामं विनियम्य समन्ततः॥
verses.transliteration:
saṅkalpaprabhavānkāmāṃstyaktvā sarvānaśeṣataḥ
manasaivendriyagrāmaṃ viniyamya samantataḥ
verses.essence: Abandoning without remainder all desires born of self-will, restraining the host of senses from every side with the mind —
verses.translation: Abandoning without reserve all desires born of Sankalpa (thought and imagination) and completely restraining the whole group of the senses by the mind from all sides.

anvaya.meaning: desires born of sankalpa
abandoning all, without remainder
by the mind alone, the troop of senses
restraining from all sides

verses.explanation: The method resumes with its hardest clause: aśeṣataḥ, without remainder — desires kept as souvenirs regrow. Their genealogy is repeated from 6.2: born of saṅkalpa, the mind's plan-making; cut the projector and the films stop. Indriya-grāma, the senses as a village — restless, many-voiced, governed only patiently and from every gate.
verses.connection: Resumes practice after the goal-vision: what 6.23's resolve actually does, hour by hour.
verses.reflection: Abandon one desire completely today — root and branch — and rein one sense by mind alone.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
शनैः शनैरुपरमेद् बुद्ध्या धृतिगृहीतया ।
आत्मसंस्थं मनः कृत्वा न किञ्चिदपि चिन्तयेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
śanaiḥ śanairuparamed buddhyā dhṛtigṛhītayā
ātmasaṃsthaṃ manaḥ kṛtvā na kiñcidapi cintayet
verses.essence: Little by little let one withdraw, with understanding held in steadiness; making the mind abide in the Self, let one think of nothing at all.
verses.translation: Little by little let him attain to ietude by the intellect held firmly; having made the mind establish itself in the Self, let him not think of anything.

anvaya.meaning: little by little let one withdraw
with the buddhi held by firmness
making the mind established in the Self
let one think of nothing whatever

verses.explanation: Two words save a thousand meditators: śanaiḥ śanaiḥ, slowly, slowly. The mind is not slammed shut but settled by degrees, the way sediment sinks in undisturbed water. Dhṛti-gṛhītayā — the understanding held by patience — and then the summit instruction, stated with disarming simplicity: think of nothing at all.
verses.connection: The tempo-marking for 6.24's restraint: gradual is not a concession but the method itself.
verses.reflection: Withdraw little by little this evening; let the mind settle in the Self and think of nothing else.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
यतो यतो निश्चरति मनश्चञ्चलमस्थिरम् ।
ततस्ततो नियम्यैतदात्मन्येव वशं नयेत्॥
verses.transliteration:
yato yato niścarati manaścañcalamasthiram
tatastato niyamyaitadātmanyeva vaśaṃ nayet
verses.essence: Wherever the restless, unsteady mind wanders out, from there reining it in, let one bring it under the sway of the Self alone.
verses.translation: From whatever cause the restless and unsteady mind wanders away, from that let him restrain it and bring it under the control of the Self alone.

anvaya.meaning: wherever (the mind) wanders out
the mind, restless, unsteady
from there, restraining it
let one lead it under the Self's control

verses.explanation: The most practically merciful verse in the chapter: yato yataḥ... tatas tataḥ — wherever, from there. The wandering is assumed, budgeted for, not condemned; the practice *is* the return, performed ten thousand times without drama. Meditation, defined here, is not the absence of distraction but the gentleness of retrieval. Yato yato nishcharati — from whatever and wherever the mind wanders, from that very place lead it back: the most practical verse in the chapter, and the one modern teachers cite as the entire technique. The commentaries stress the tone: niyamya, restrain — but etad vasham nayet, lead it home, like a calf, not a criminal. The returning is the meditation; the wandering merely supplies occasions.
verses.connection: The working loop inside 6.25's slowness: stray, notice, return — the whole technique.
verses.reflection: Each time attention wanders today, bring it back without commentary — that returning is the exercise.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
प्रशान्तमनसं ह्येनं योगिनं सुखमुत्तमम् ।
उपैति शान्तरजसं ब्रह्मभूतमकल्मषम्॥
verses.transliteration:
praśāntamanasaṃ hyenaṃ yoginaṃ sukhamuttamam
upaiti śāntarajasaṃ brahmabhūtamakalmaṣam
verses.essence: Supreme bliss comes to this yogi of peaceful mind, with passion stilled — become Brahman, free of stain.
verses.translation: Supreme Bliss verily comes to this Yogi whose mind is ite peaceful, whose passion is ieted, who has become Brahman and who is free from sin.

anvaya.meaning: for this one of peaceful mind
to this yogi, the highest happiness
comes — whose rajas is calmed
become Brahman, stainless

verses.explanation: Note the verb's direction: upaiti — the bliss *comes to* the yogi. Happiness was never manufactured by the practice; the practice only quieted the turbulence (śānta-rajasam) that kept it at bay. Still the water, and the moon that was always overhead appears in it.
verses.connection: The fruit of 6.26's patient returns: what arrives on its own when the chasing stops.
verses.reflection: Catch the supreme happiness that visits a quieted mind — even thirty calm seconds carry the signature.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
युञ्जन्नेवं सदात्मानं योगी विगतकल्मषः ।
सुखेन ब्रह्मसंस्पर्शमत्यन्तं सुखमश्नुते॥
verses.transliteration:
yuñjannevaṃ sadātmānaṃ yogī vigatakalmaṣaḥ
sukhena brahmasaṃsparśamatyantaṃ sukhamaśnute
verses.essence: Thus ever harnessing the self, the yogi freed from stain easily enjoys the boundless bliss of contact with Brahman.
verses.translation: The Yogi, always engaging the mind thus (in the practice of Yoga), freed from sins, easily enjoys the Infinite Bliss of contact with Brahman (the Eternal).

anvaya.meaning: thus always yoking the self
the yogi with impurities gone
with ease, the touch of Brahman
the boundless bliss, enjoys

verses.explanation: Two words elevate this beyond 6.27: sukhena, *easily* — the struggle phase ends; practice becomes the path of least resistance — and brahma-saṁsparśa, the *touch* of Brahman. Not inference, not belief: contact, the most intimate word the body knows, borrowed for the soul's encounter with the Absolute.
verses.connection: Completes the inner arc: the discipline of 6.24–26 matured into effortless communion.
verses.reflection: Touch the boundless once today — through breath, prayer, or silence — and let it perfume the hours after.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थमात्मानं सर्वभूतानि चात्मनि ।
ईक्षते योगयुक्तात्मा सर्वत्र समदर्शनः॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūtasthamātmānaṃ sarvabhūtāni cātmani
īkṣate yogayuktātmā sarvatra samadarśanaḥ
verses.essence: One harnessed in yoga, seeing the same everywhere, sees the Self present in all beings and all beings in the Self.
verses.translation: With the mind harmonised by Yoga he sees the Self abiding in all beings and all beings in the Self; he sees the same everywhere.

anvaya.meaning: the Self abiding in all beings
and all beings in the Self
beholds the one whose self is yoked in yoga
everywhere seeing the same

verses.explanation: The eyes open, and the meditation's fruit turns out to be a way of seeing the world: one Self threaded through every being, every being held within the one Self — both directions at once. Sama-darśana returns from 5.18, now not as ethics practiced but as perception delivered. Sarva-bhuta-stham atmanam — the self in all beings, all beings in the self: the equal-vision verse the Upanishads (Isha 6) speak almost verbatim. Commentators call this the fruit by which the practice is audited: hours on the seat are validated not by visions but by whether the gaze, rising, finds the same one everywhere. Sarvatra sama-darshanah — the cushion's diploma is issued in the street.
verses.connection: The pivot outward: 6.28's inner touch becomes 6.29–32's universal vision.
verses.schools_note: 'Sarva-bhuta-stham atmanam' restates Isha Upanishad 6, and the schools sign it differently: Advaita — one Self, literally identical in all; Vishishtadvaita — the same Lord ensouling every soul, likeness amid real plurality; Dvaita — the vision of God's presence in all, beings remaining distinct. The practice all derive is the same audit: meditation is verified by how one sees the street.
verses.reflection: Look at one stranger today and silently note: the same Self there as here. Equal vision, one rep.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
यो मां पश्यति सर्वत्र सर्वं च मयि पश्यति ।
तस्याहं न प्रणश्यामि स च मे न प्रणश्यति॥
verses.transliteration:
yo māṃ paśyati sarvatra sarvaṃ ca mayi paśyati
tasyāhaṃ na praṇaśyāmi sa ca me na praṇaśyati
verses.essence: One who sees me everywhere and sees everything in me — I am never lost to that one, nor is that one ever lost to me.
verses.translation: He who sees Me everywhere and sees everything in Me, he never becomes separated from Me, nor do I become separated from him.

anvaya.meaning: who sees me everywhere
and sees all in me
to that one I am not lost
and that one is not lost to me

verses.explanation: The chapter's most tender promise, and it is mutual: na praṇaśyāmi — I do not vanish for him, he does not vanish for me. Whoever has learned to find Krishna in all things can no longer lose him in any circumstance; and such a seer is held, never dropped, never out of sight. Two gazes, permanently locked. Yo mam pashyati sarvatra — and here the impersonal vision gains a face and a vow: who sees Me everywhere, and everything in Me — tasyaham na pranashyami, I am never lost to him, nor he to Me. The theistic schools treasure this as meditation's secret signature: the all-pervading turns out to be Someone, and the someone makes a promise of mutual unlosability.
verses.connection: Personalizes 6.29: the universal Self acquires a face, and the seeing becomes a relationship.
verses.schools_note: The mutual-unlosability pledge — 'I am not lost to him, nor is he lost to Me' — is the theistic schools' treasure: Ramanuja reads it as the Lord's covenant with the seer of unity; the Gaudiyas as the eternal bond made explicit. Shankara reads the non-loss as the impossibility of separation from one's own Self. Either way it is the Gita's strongest sentence against the fear of abandonment.
verses.reflection: Hold the promise close in a lonely hour: see Him everywhere, and you are never lost to Him.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
सर्वभूतस्थितं यो मां भजत्येकत्वमास्थितः ।
सर्वथा वर्तमानोऽपि स योगी मयि वर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
sarvabhūtasthitaṃ yo māṃ bhajatyekatvamāsthitaḥ
sarvathā vartamāno'pi sa yogī mayi vartate
verses.essence: The yogi who, established in oneness, worships me as dwelling in all beings — that one abides in me, whatever the manner of living.
verses.translation: He who, being established in unity, worships Me Who dwells in all beings, that Yogi abides in Me, whatever may be his mode of living.

anvaya.meaning: me, abiding in all beings, who
worships, established in oneness
in whatever way living
that yogi dwells in me

verses.explanation: Sarvathā vartamāno 'pi — however such a one happens to be living: in a forest or a market, in robes or armor. Once worship has become the perception of the One in all, lifestyle ceases to be the measure; the address is fixed — mayi vartate, dwelling in me — regardless of the outer route.
verses.connection: Extends 6.30's mutual sight into mutual residence, dissolving the monk-versus-householder question.
verses.reflection: Worship through your situation today, whatever it is — the one-in-all accepts every mode of living.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.devanagari:
आत्मौपम्येन सर्वत्र समं पश्यति योऽर्जुन ।
सुखं वा यदि वा दुःखं स योगी परमो मतः॥
verses.transliteration:
ātmaupamyena sarvatra samaṃ paśyati yo'rjuna
sukhaṃ vā yadi vā duḥkhaṃ sa yogī paramo mataḥ
verses.essence: One who, by the analogy of the self, sees the same everywhere — in joy or in sorrow — is deemed the highest yogi, Arjuna.
verses.translation: He who, through the likeness of the Self, O Arjuna, sees eality everywhere, be it pleasure or pain, he is regarded as the highest Yogi.

anvaya.meaning: by the likeness of oneself, everywhere
who sees the same, O Arjuna
whether joy or sorrow
that yogi is deemed the highest

verses.explanation: The vision's ethical edge: ātmaupamya, taking oneself as the measure — their pain weighed on the scale of my pain, their joy on the scale of mine. The golden rule, but grounded in metaphysics rather than prudence: the other's suffering matters like mine because, at the depth just seen, the other *is* mine. This empathy is crowned paramaḥ — the highest yoga of all.
verses.connection: Caps the vision-section: the summit of meditation is measured, finally, in compassion.
verses.reflection: Use your own pain as the translator today: feel one person's joy or sorrow as if it were yours.
verses.background: The practice and its fruit are pressed home: renounce every desire without remainder, withdraw little by little, and from wherever the restless mind wanders, lead it back. The fruit is the equal vision — the Self in all beings, all in the Self — and the mutual promise: who sees Me everywhere is never lost to Me.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
योऽयं योगस्त्वया प्रोक्तः साम्येन मधुसूदन ।
एतस्याहं न पश्यामि चञ्चलत्वात्स्थितिं स्थिराम्॥
verses.transliteration:
yo'yaṃ yogastvayā proktaḥ sāmyena madhusūdana
etasyāhaṃ na paśyāmi cañcalatvātsthitiṃ sthirām
verses.essence: Arjuna says: this yoga of evenness you have taught, Madhusudana — because of restlessness, I do not see how it can stand firm.
verses.translation: This Yoga of eanimity taught by Thee, O Krishna, I do not see its steady continuance, because of the restlessness (of the mind).

anvaya.meaning: this yoga which you have declared
as evenness, O Madhusudana
of this I do not see
because of restlessness, a steady standing

verses.explanation: After thirty-two verses of luminous instruction, the student raises his hand with the objection every meditator owns: it sounds beautiful, but my mind won't hold it. Arjuna's honesty rescues the chapter from idealism — the lamp may be windless in theory; his is guttering.
verses.connection: The voice of every practitioner interrupting 6.29–32's heights with the floor's-eye view.
verses.reflection: Admit your restlessness honestly, as Arjuna did — diagnosis spoken aloud is half the discipline.
verses.background: Arjuna interrupts with the most relatable objection in the poem: this yoga of evenness — he cannot see it standing steady, for the mind is restless, turbulent, strong, obstinate; restraining it seems as hard as restraining the wind.

verses.devanagari:
चञ्चलं हि मनः कृष्ण प्रमाथि बलवद् दृढम् ।
तस्याहं निग्रहं मन्ये वायोरिव सुदुष्करम्॥
verses.transliteration:
cañcalaṃ hi manaḥ kṛṣṇa pramāthi balavad dṛḍham
tasyāhaṃ nigrahaṃ manye vāyoriva suduṣkaram
verses.essence: For the mind is restless, Krishna — turbulent, strong, obstinate. To restrain it, I think, is as hard as restraining the wind.
verses.translation: The mind verily is restless, turbulent, strong and unyielding, O Krishna: I deem it as difficult to control it as to control the wind.

anvaya.meaning: for restless is the mind, O Krishna
turbulent, strong, obstinate
its restraint I consider
like the wind's — exceedingly hard

verses.explanation: Four adjectives, each worse than the last: restless, then pramāthi — churning, actively agitating its owner — then strong, then dṛḍham, set in its ways. The simile is a warrior's: he has bent bows and broken armies, but the wind one cannot grip. The complaint is three thousand years old and not a day stale.
verses.connection: Completes 6.33's objection with the diagnosis every age has recognized as its own.
verses.reflection: Watch the mind for one minute and respect the opponent: turbulent, strong, obstinate — and trainable.
verses.background: Arjuna interrupts with the most relatable objection in the poem: this yoga of evenness — he cannot see it standing steady, for the mind is restless, turbulent, strong, obstinate; restraining it seems as hard as restraining the wind.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
असंशयं महाबाहो मनो दुर्निग्रहं चलम् ।
अभ्यासेन तु कौन्तेय वैराग्येण च गृह्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
asaṃśayaṃ mahābāho mano durnigrahaṃ calam
abhyāsena tu kaunteya vairāgyeṇa ca gṛhyate
verses.essence: Doubtless, mighty-armed one, the mind is hard to restrain and restless. But by practice, Kaunteya, and by dispassion, it is held.
verses.translation: Undoubtedly, O mighty-armed Arjuna, the mind is difficult to control and restless; but by practice and by dispassion it may be restrained.

anvaya.meaning: without doubt, O mighty-armed
the mind is hard to restrain, fickle
but by practice, O Kaunteya
and by dispassion, it is grasped

verses.explanation: First, the concession — asaṁśayam, no argument: you are right about the wind. Then the two-word cure that all later yoga inherits: abhyāsa, repetition that wears a channel, and vairāgya, the cooling of the cravings that supply the wind in the first place. Effort on one side, release on the other — held between them, the mind settles. Abhyasena tu kaunteya vairagyena cha — by practice and by dispassion: the two-word prescription that Patanjali's Yoga-sutra (1.12) states identically, the two traditions countersigning each other. The commentaries explain the pairing: practice alone grows tense, dispassion alone goes limp; together they are the two wings — asamshayam, without doubt, the mind is caught.
verses.connection: The classical answer to 6.34, adopted verbatim by Patanjali as yoga's twin pillars.
verses.reflection: Use both reins today: practice when fresh, dispassion when tempted — abhyasa and vairagya together.
verses.background: Krishna concedes the difficulty without surrendering the goal: doubtless the mind is hard to curb, O mighty-armed — but it is caught by practice and dispassion. For the uncontrolled, yoga is hard; for the striving and self-governed, attainable.

verses.devanagari:
असंयतात्मना योगो दुष्प्राप इति मे मतिः ।
वश्यात्मना तु यतता शक्योऽवाप्तुमुपायतः॥
verses.transliteration:
asaṃyatātmanā yogo duṣprāpa iti me matiḥ
vaśyātmanā tu yatatā śakyo'vāptumupāyataḥ
verses.essence: For one of unrestrained self, yoga is hard to attain — this is my view. But for the self-governed who strives, it can be won by right means.
verses.translation: I think Yoga is hard to be attained by one of uncontrolled self, but the self-controlled and striving one can attain to it by the (proper) means.

anvaya.meaning: by one of unrestrained self, yoga
is hard to attain — such is my view
but by the self-controlled, striving
it is possible to attain, by means

verses.explanation: Honest pricing: duṣprāpa, genuinely hard — the Gita does not discount the difficulty to make a sale. But the hardness is conditional, not absolute, and the final word carries the hope: upāyataḥ, by method. What will cannot storm, technique can win; the gate is locked, and there is a key.
verses.connection: Seals the exchange of 6.33–35: difficulty conceded, impossibility denied.
verses.reflection: Stop demanding self-mastery in one leap; strive by the right means today and call that success.
verses.background: Krishna concedes the difficulty without surrendering the goal: doubtless the mind is hard to curb, O mighty-armed — but it is caught by practice and dispassion. For the uncontrolled, yoga is hard; for the striving and self-governed, attainable.

verses.speaker: अर्जुन उवाच
verses.devanagari:
अयतिः श्रद्धयोपेतो योगाच्चलितमानसः ।
अप्राप्य योगसंसिद्धिं कां गतिं कृष्ण गच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
ayatiḥ śraddhayopeto yogāccalitamānasaḥ
aprāpya yogasaṃsiddhiṃ kāṃ gatiṃ kṛṣṇa gacchati
verses.essence: Arjuna asks: one who has faith but insufficient effort, whose mind falls from yoga before reaching perfection — what way does such a one go, Krishna?
verses.translation: He who is unable to control himself though he has the faith, and whose mind wanders away from Yoga, what end does he, having failed to attain perfection in Yoga, mee,t O Krishna?

anvaya.meaning: the uncontrolled one, endowed with faith
whose mind has strayed from yoga
not having attained yoga's perfection
what way, O Krishna, does he go

verses.explanation: The bravest question in the chapter: what about the one who tried and failed? Faith intact, effort insufficient, journey unfinished — death arrives mid-path. Arjuna is not asking abstractly; a man about to attempt the hardest discipline wants to know the cost of falling short.
verses.connection: Opens the great consolation passage (6.37–45): the fate of the sincere but incomplete.
verses.reflection: Bring your honest fear of failing the path into the open — the question itself is held kindly.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the question for every imperfect seeker: what becomes of the one who has faith but strays, who fails to reach perfection — does he perish like a riven cloud, fallen from both worlds? He begs Krishna to cut this doubt completely.

verses.devanagari:
कच्चिन्नोभयविभ्रष्टश्छिन्नाभ्रमिव नश्यति ।
अप्रतिष्ठो महाबाहो विमूढो ब्रह्मणः पथि॥
verses.transliteration:
kaccinnobhayavibhraṣṭaśchinnābhramiva naśyati
apratiṣṭho mahābāho vimūḍho brahmaṇaḥ pathi
verses.essence: Fallen from both, does he not perish like a riven cloud — without support, bewildered on the path to Brahman?
verses.translation: Swami Sivananda did not comment on this sloka

anvaya.meaning: does he not, fallen from both
perish like a rent cloud
without footing, O mighty-armed
bewildered on the path of Brahman

verses.explanation: The fear given its full image: ubhaya-vibhraṣṭa, fallen from both worlds — having renounced the worldly race, yet missing the spiritual finish — like a small cloud torn from its bank, dissolving in empty sky, belonging nowhere. It is the secret terror of everyone who has ever left a safe shore for an uncertain crossing.
verses.connection: Intensifies 6.37: the double loss feared by all who stake ordinary success on extraordinary hope.
verses.reflection: When you feel between two worlds — committed to neither — remember the question was asked for you.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the question for every imperfect seeker: what becomes of the one who has faith but strays, who fails to reach perfection — does he perish like a riven cloud, fallen from both worlds? He begs Krishna to cut this doubt completely.

verses.devanagari:
एतन्मे संशयं कृष्ण छेत्तुमर्हस्यशेषतः ।
त्वदन्यः संशयस्यास्य छेत्ता न ह्युपपद्यते॥
verses.transliteration:
etanme saṃśayaṃ kṛṣṇa chettumarhasyaśeṣataḥ
tvadanyaḥ saṃśayasyāsya chettā na hyupapadyate
verses.essence: This doubt of mine, Krishna, please dispel without remainder — for none but you can be the destroyer of this doubt.
verses.translation: This doubt of mine, O Krishna, do Thou dispel completely; because it is not possible for any but Thee to dispel this doubt.

anvaya.meaning: this doubt of mine, O Krishna
you should cut, without remainder
other than you, of this doubt
a cutter is not to be found

verses.explanation: Arjuna asks for surgery, not reassurance: chettum, cut it — aśeṣataḥ, leaving no root to regrow. And he names why he asks here: on the question of what happens to the failed soul, only the one who oversees souls can answer. Some doubts outrank every human authority.
verses.connection: Hands 6.37–38's fear to the only court competent to rule on it.
verses.reflection: Take your unresolvable doubt to the highest court you know today, and actually wait for the answer.
verses.background: Arjuna asks the question for every imperfect seeker: what becomes of the one who has faith but strays, who fails to reach perfection — does he perish like a riven cloud, fallen from both worlds? He begs Krishna to cut this doubt completely.

verses.devanagari:
श्रीभगवानुवाच ।
पार्थ नैवेह नामुत्र विनाशस्तस्य विद्यते ।
न हि कल्याणकृत्कश्चिद् दुर्गतिं तात गच्छति॥
verses.transliteration:
pārtha naiveha nāmutra vināśastasya vidyate
na hi kalyāṇakṛtkaścid durgatiṃ tāta gacchati
verses.essence: Partha, neither here nor hereafter is there destruction for him. No one who does good, dear friend, ever comes to grief.
verses.translation: O Arjuna, neither in this world, nor in the next world is there destruction for him; none, verily, who does good, O My son, ever comes to grief.

anvaya.word: पार्थ न एव इह न अमुत्र
anvaya.meaning: O Partha, neither here nor hereafter
is there destruction for him
for no doer of good, anyone
goes to an evil end, dear one

verses.explanation: The answer opens with the rarest word Krishna uses: tāta — dear child, a father's address. Then the universal amnesty: na hi kalyāṇa-kṛt kaścid durgatiṁ gacchati — no one who has done a good thing, ever, travels to a bad end. In this cosmos, sincere effort is a deposit that cannot be confiscated; the universe keeps honest books. Na hi kalyana-krit kashchid durgatim tata gachchhati — the Gita's amnesty verse, spoken with the tender address tata, 'dear child.' No doer of good ever comes to grief — not in this world, not beyond. Commentators call it the abolition of spiritual ruin: the path keeps every deposit, honors every partial effort, and fears no incomplete life. It is the verse most often spoken at deathbeds of unfinished seekers.
verses.connection: The thunderclap of consolation answering 6.38's torn cloud: nothing offered upward is lost.
verses.reflection: Engrave the amnesty somewhere visible: no one who does good ever comes to a bad end.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
प्राप्य पुण्यकृतां लोकानुषित्वा शाश्वतीः समाः ।
शुचीनां श्रीमतां गेहे योगभ्रष्टोऽभिजायते॥
verses.transliteration:
prāpya puṇyakṛtāṃ lokānuṣitvā śāśvatīḥ samāḥ
śucīnāṃ śrīmatāṃ gehe yogabhraṣṭo'bhijāyate
verses.essence: Having reached the worlds of the righteous and dwelt there for long years, the one fallen from yoga is born in the home of the pure and prosperous.
verses.translation: Having attained to the worlds of the righteous and having dwelt there for everlasting years, he who fell from Yoga is rorn in a house of the pure and wealthy.

anvaya.meaning: having attained the worlds of the righteous
having dwelt for everlasting years
in the home of the pure, the prosperous
the one fallen from yoga is born

verses.explanation: The itinerary of the unfinished: first rest — long ages in luminous worlds, the effort honored — then re-entry, and not at random: a household clean in conduct and free of want, soil chosen for the seed. The interrupted journey resumes with better provisions than it had.
verses.connection: Begins detailing 6.40's promise: failure rerouted, never erased.
verses.reflection: Trust that your past efforts are banked — you may be living, right now, off a forgotten deposit.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
अथवा योगिनामेव कुले भवति धीमताम् ।
एतद्धि दुर्लभतरं लोके जन्म यदीदृशम्॥
verses.transliteration:
athavā yogināmeva kule bhavati dhīmatām
etaddhi durlabhataraṃ loke janma yadīdṛśam
verses.essence: Or one is born into a family of wise yogis themselves — such a birth as this is exceedingly rare in the world.
verses.translation: Or he is born in a family of even the wise Yogis; verily a birth like this is very difficult to obtain in this world.

anvaya.meaning: or else, of yogis themselves
in the family, one is born — of the wise
for this is rarer still
in the world, a birth such as this

verses.explanation: The express route for the further-along: birth straight into a lineage of practitioners, where the path is the family business and the atmosphere itself instructs. Durlabhataram — rarer than the rare: the tradition prized being born among the wise above being born among the wealthy.
verses.connection: The second landing-site after 6.41, graded to how far the traveler had come.
verses.reflection: If wisdom feels strangely native to you, honor the rarity — and do not waste the head start.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
तत्र तं बुद्धिसंयोगं लभते पौर्वदेहिकम् ।
यतते च ततो भूयः संसिद्धौ कुरुनन्दन॥
verses.transliteration:
tatra taṃ buddhisaṃyogaṃ labhate paurvadehikam
yatate ca tato bhūyaḥ saṃsiddhau kurunandana
verses.essence: There one regains the union of understanding earned in the former body, and strives onward again toward perfection, Kurunandana.
verses.translation: Thee he comes in touch with the knowledge acired in his former body and strives more than before for perfection, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: there, that union of buddhi
one obtains, belonging to the former body
and strives, thence, further
toward complete perfection, O joy of the Kurus

verses.explanation: The mechanics of continuity: paurva-dehikam, the prior body's cultivated understanding, restored like a file reopened. Nothing learned in the inner life had evaporated; it waited. Hence the unexplainable affinities of childhood — the pull toward silence, scripture, or the sacred that arrives before any teaching could have planted it.
verses.connection: Explains how 6.41–42's new births resume rather than restart the journey.
verses.reflection: Notice what draws you even against convenience — previous practice is pulling. Let it.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
पूर्वाभ्यासेन तेनैव ह्रियते ह्यवशोऽपि सः ।
जिज्ञासुरपि योगस्य शब्दब्रह्मातिवर्तते॥
verses.transliteration:
pūrvābhyāsena tenaiva hriyate hyavaśo'pi saḥ
jijñāsurapi yogasya śabdabrahmātivartate
verses.essence: By that very former practice one is carried onward, even despite oneself. Even the mere inquirer into yoga passes beyond the Veda's word.
verses.translation: By that very former practice he is borne on in spite of himself. Even he who merely wishes to know Yoga goes beyond the Brahmic word.

anvaya.meaning: by that very former practice
one is carried, even involuntarily
even the (mere) inquirer of yoga
passes beyond the word-Brahman

verses.explanation: Hriyate avaśo 'pi — carried, even unwilling: old practice becomes a current under the boat, momentum that no longer requires the rower's consent. And a stunning aside: even the jijñāsu, the one who has merely begun *asking* about yoga, already outruns śabda-brahma, religion-as-formula. The question outranks the recitation.
verses.connection: Completes the consolation's logic: practice compounds across lives, and even curiosity counts.
verses.reflection: Honor the seeker's restlessness in you; even the wish to know already outruns ritual.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
प्रयत्नाद्यतमानस्तु योगी संशुद्धकिल्बिषः ।
अनेकजन्मसंसिद्धस्ततो याति परां गतिम्॥
verses.transliteration:
prayatnādyatamānastu yogī saṃśuddhakilbiṣaḥ
anekajanmasaṃsiddhastato yāti parāṃ gatim
verses.essence: But the yogi striving with effort, cleansed of stain, perfected through many births, then reaches the supreme goal.
verses.translation: But the Yogi who strives with assiduity, purified of sins and perfected gradually through many births, reaches the highest goal.

anvaya.meaning: but striving with effort
the yogi, thoroughly cleansed of taint
perfected through many births
then goes to the supreme goal

verses.explanation: The honest timescale: aneka-janma-saṁsiddhaḥ — perfected across many births. The path may be longer than a lifetime, and the Gita declines to pretend otherwise; what it removes is not the length but the fear of the length. Every step banks; no birth starts from zero; the arrival, however delayed, is arithmetic.
verses.connection: Seals 6.37–44: the failed yogi's question answered with a multi-life guarantee.
verses.reflection: Take the long view of your striving tonight: many lives of polishing, then the goal. No panic.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
तपस्विभ्योऽधिको योगी ज्ञानिभ्योऽपि मतोऽधिकः ।
कर्मिभ्यश्चाधिको योगी तस्माद्योगी भवार्जुन॥
verses.transliteration:
tapasvibhyo'dhiko yogī jñānibhyo'pi mato'dhikaḥ
karmibhyaścādhiko yogī tasmādyogī bhavārjuna
verses.essence: The yogi is greater than ascetics, deemed greater even than the knowers, and greater than the doers of works. Therefore be a yogi, Arjuna.
verses.translation: The Yogi is thought to be superior to the ascetics and even superior to men of knowledge (obtained through the study of scriptures); he is also superior to men of action; therefore be thou a Yogi, O Arjuna.

anvaya.meaning: greater than ascetics is the yogi
deemed greater even than the knowers
and greater than the workers, the yogi
therefore be a yogi, Arjuna

verses.explanation: Three honored vocations measured and ranked below one: above the tapasvī who masters the body, the jñānī of the texts, the karmī of the rites stands the yogi — the one in whom discipline, knowledge and action have fused into inward union. Then the chapter's whole teaching compressed into three words of direct address: tasmād yogī bhava.
verses.connection: The penultimate verse crowns the path — before the final verse crowns its summit.
verses.reflection: Choose the yogi's path over the ascetic's, the scholar's, the ritualist's — be one, today, in spirit.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.

verses.devanagari:
योगिनामपि सर्वेषां मद्गतेनान्तरात्मना ।
श्रद्धावान्भजते यो मां स मे युक्ततमो मतः॥
verses.transliteration:
yogināmapi sarveṣāṃ madgatenāntarātmanā
śraddhāvānbhajate yo māṃ sa me yuktatamo mataḥ
verses.essence: And of all yogis, the one who worships me with faith, the inner self merged in me — that one I hold the most harnessed of all.
verses.translation: And among all the Yogis he who, full of faith and with his inner self merged in Me, worships Me is deemed by Me to be the most devout.

anvaya.meaning: even of all yogis
with the inner self gone into me
who, full of faith, worships me
that one is deemed by me the most yoked

verses.explanation: One rung above 6.46's summit: among yogis themselves, the yuktatama — the most-united — is the one whose interior has emigrated into Krishna (mad-gatena antarātmanā) and who worships with faith. The chapter that began with posture and breath ends with love: technique was the riverbed; devotion is the river. Shraddhavan bhajate yo mam — the chapter's last word crowns, above all ascetics, knowers, and workers, the one who loves with inner self gone to Me. The commentaries mark the structural shock: six chapters of discipline resolve into devotion, and the word yukta-tamah — most yoked — is awarded to intimacy. The bridge to the bhakti chapters is this single superlative.
verses.connection: The chapter's last word quietly hands the crown from meditation to bhakti — the bridge into chapters 7–12.
verses.schools_note: A hinge verse for the tradition: Ramanuja and the bhakti schools read it as devotion's formal supremacy over all paths; Shankara as the meditator on the Lord being foremost among practitioners. Either way, the dhyana chapter deliberately ends pointing beyond technique.
verses.reflection: Of all your practices, let one today be done with inmost self gone to Him — the highest yoga.
verses.background: Krishna's answer is the Gita's great amnesty, spoken with the tenderness of 'dear child': no doer of good ever comes to grief. The fallen yogi rises in blessed homes or yogic families, drawn on by old practice, perfected across births — and the chapter crowns, above ascetics and scholars, the yogi who loves.
